coredata.dc:title: Luminescence turn-off and turn-on interaction mechanism of optical probe with hydrogen peroxide
coredata.dc:creator: Bishnoi, Swati
Acharyya, Jitendra Nath
Prakash, G. Vijaya
Pattanayek, Sudip K.
coredata.dc:description: The mechanism of interaction between luminescent positively charged Carbon dots (CDs) and manganese dioxide (MnO2) based optical probes and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has been investigated. The optical probe has the mechanism of luminescence turn-off and on, which enables the detection of H2O2. MnO2 nanosheets can quench CDs luminescence due to spectral overlap between CDs emission and MnO2 absorption. As a result of the underlying detailed redox reaction mechanism, the initial quenched probe exhibits luminescence activation upon exposure to H2O2 solution. The quenched probe begins to regain the luminescence that increases as the concentration of H2O2 increases (0.1–12μ M). The Johnson-Mehl-Avrami-Kolmogorov model was used to investigate the luminescence turn-on mechanism further.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescence
MnO2 nanosheets
Positively charged carbon dots
Turn-on probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
Characterization of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author SB would like to acknowledge IITD for providing Postdoctoral fellowship. JNA acknowledges the DST-INSPIRE fellowship. The authors are grateful to the Central Research facility (CRF), IIT Delhi for TEM and XRD facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram showing luminescence turn-off and turn-on mechanism in CD: MnO2 optical probe.

Structural and morphological characterization of optical probe components (a) XRD pattern of carbon dots (b) Dynamic light scattering measurement of CDs (c) TEM micrograph of as synthesised- CDs (d) FTIR analysis of CDs.
(a) UV-VIS absorbance spectra of CD (b) PL emission spectra of CD. (c) UV-VIS absorbance spectra of MnO2 nanosheets (d) XRD spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets.

TEM images (a) & (b) single layer MnO2 nanosheets. Inset of 4(b) shows the SAED pattern of single layer MnO2 nanosheets. (c) & (d) TEM micrographs of CD: MnO2 optical probe, showing interaction between the two.

Optical properties of various components of probe (a) Combined UV-VIS absorbance (MnO2) and PL emission (CD) spectra showing spectral overlap. (b) PL emission spectra showing quenching mechanism in the CD: MnO2 optical probe on addition of MnO2 in CD (c) Combined UV-VIS absorption spectra of CD, CD: MnO2 and MnO2.

(a) Fluorescence restoration of the C-dot–MnO2 nanosheet optical probe upon addition of various concentrations of H2O2 (λex = 405 nm), (b) The plot of normalized PL peak intensity (with respect to the maximum concentration) versus concentrations of H2O2. The solid line represents the theoretical fit using Johnson−Mehl−Avrami−Kolmogorov (JMAK) model and shaded region indicate the scope of error. (c) The corresponding optical images at various concentrations under UV excitation.
Comparative PL turn-on emission intensity for H2O2, HCl and H2SO4.

head.ce:title: Luminescence turn-off and turn-on interaction mechanism of optical probe with hydrogen peroxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Delhi, 110016, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Delhi, 110016, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanophotonics Lab, Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, New Delhi, 110016, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanophotonics Lab, Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, New Delhi 110016, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The mechanism of interaction between luminescent positively charged Carbon dots (CDs) and manganese dioxide (MnO2) based optical probes and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has been investigated. The optical probe has the mechanism of luminescence turn-off and on, which enables the detection of H2O2. MnO2 nanosheets can quench CDs luminescence due to spectral overlap between CDs emission and MnO2 absorption. As a result of the underlying detailed redox reaction mechanism, the initial quenched probe exhibits luminescence activation upon exposure to H2O2 solution. The quenched probe begins to regain the luminescence that increases as the concentration of H2O2 increases (0.1–12μ M). The Johnson-Mehl-Avrami-Kolmogorov model was used to investigate the luminescence turn-on mechanism further.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The sensing of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), a crucial by-product of metabolic processes, is attracting great research interest worldwide [1–3]. The dysregulation of H2O2 concentration in the human body may have deleterious effects on the brain or tissues. It may indicate the presence of underlying diseases such as cancer, Alzheimer's, myocardial infarction, atherosclerosis, or other cardiovascular diseases [4–6]. The abnormal increase in H2O2 concentration in cancerous cells makes it a potential biomarker for detecting cancer [7]. In addition, H2O2, a powerful redox agent, has multidisciplinary applications such as in food, medical, chemical, and environmental detection. According to a recent study, H2O2 concentrations above 75 ppm can negatively impact human health, leading to the development of simple, accurate, and dependable methods for H2O2 detection [8].

Presently, a variety of analytical techniques have been explored for H2O2 detection, including Electroluminescence [9,10], Electrochemical [11,12], Spectrophotometry [13,14], Chromatography [15,16], Fluorescence [17,18], etc. The widely used detection method for H2O2 is the enzyme-based method, which has a high cost, sensitivity to environmental conditions, short shelf-life, and complicated immobilization procedures [19–21]. These drawbacks restrict their commercial application and lead to the emergence of enzyme-free sensors for H2O2 detection. The luminescence-based sensing is the most suited method due to its multiple advantages, such as short response time, improved selectivity, minimum preparation, rapid response, high sensitivity, and simple manipulation [22,23].

Most luminescence-based sensors rely on the quenching mechanism. However, the luminescence turn-on-based detection is superior to quenching as it reduces the likelihood of false-positive signals caused by co-existing quenchers and increases the overall sensitivity [24,25]. This led to the emergence of luminescence turn-on-based sensors, which are more reliable for detection applications. Several research groups have reported efficient sensing of various analytes with this luminescence turn-on mechanism [26–28]. The reported luminescence-based H2O2 sensing is based on a single-step quenching mechanism [29–33]. Previously, Gao et al. utilized instinct peroxidase-like property of Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) for detecting H2O2, based on quenching of cadmium, telluride quantum dots [34]. Among the turn-on-based detection studies of H2O2, silver nanoparticle (AgNP)-mediated quenching of a luminescent Ir(III) complex (Ir-1) was studied [35].

The luminescence-based optical probe system should have nanomaterials with appreciable absorption and emission property for it to be successful. In the present work, we have explored the underlying interaction mechanism of a cost-effective optical probe comprising positively charged carbon dots (CDs) and negatively charged MnO2 nanosheets with H2O2. CDs, an environment-friendly fluorescent material, are used as a fluorescent probe in the field of analysis and detection owing to their advantages of high quantum yield, stable fluorescence properties, simple synthesis, and low toxicity [36,37]. On the other hand, MnO2 nanosheets are comparatively large-sized nanomaterials exhibiting high stability, large surface area, wide absorption range, and easy synthesis [38,39]. This Thereby makes them one of the best choices as luminescence quenchers. We note that CDs were used as the fluorescent probe, MnO2 nanosheets were used as the fluorescence quencher in the formulated optical probe.

The synthesis chemistry of positively charged CDs and MnO2 nanosheets have been discussed in detail. Thereafter, the CDs: MnO2 nanocomposite probe was formulated by mixing CDs and MnO2 nanosheets. The as-synthesised CDs were highly luminescent with a broad emission in the visible range. However, after mixing CDs and MnO2, subsequent quenching was observed in the blend, and no resultant CD emission was observed. This quenching can be attributed to the spectral overlapping between CD emission and MnO2 nanosheets absorption, which results in Froster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [40] (Fig. 1 ). The other possible mechanism could be the absorption competition mechanism, which usually occurs when the absorption range of two compounds shows significant overlapping [41,42].

Afterward, on subsequent addition of the H2O2 (varying molar concentration 0.1–12 μM) there occurs, the breakdown of MnO2 nanosheets into smaller fragments, which weakened the absorbance of MnO2 nanosheets and resulted in luminescence turn on. Also, on addition of H2O2 there occurs a redox reaction, which leads to whereas the MnO2 nano-sheets were broken down and Mn4+ was rapidly reduced into colourless Mn2+ [43–45]. This dissolution reaction breakdown the MnO2 nanosheets and thereby, the CDs emission started recovering, on H2O2 addition in the blend. Moreover, we have considered the opposite surface charge of optical probe materials (CDs and MnO2) to ensure better interaction between the two. The detailed synthesis chemistry of CDs and MnO2 have been discussed and supported with underlying chemical reactions. Besides this, we have also investigated the underlying interaction mechanism between the optical probe and H2O2, resulting in luminescence turn-on. Furthermore, the Johnson-Mehl-Avrami- Kolmogorov (JMAK) model has been used to study the underlying chemical transformation.

Malic acid, melamine, Polyethylenimine (PEI), Potassium Permanganate (KMnO4), Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), and ethanol were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich company. All chemicals were analytically compatible and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: The positively charged CDs were synthesised using the hydrothermal method [46]. This method uses Malic acid as a major carbon precursor and Polyethylenimine (PEI) as a nitrogen source. PEI increases the surface nitrogen content, resulting in enhanced luminescence, and imparts a positive charge to the surface to ensure interaction with negatively charged MnO2 nanosheets via electrostatic interaction, resulting in a more sensitive probe formulation. For the synthesis of CDs, 1 g of malic acid and 1 g of PEI were dissolved in 26 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for several minutes. The solution mentioned above was added to 100 mL of autoclave and heated for 10 h at 180 °C in an oven. The resulting solution was repeatedly washed, filtered with a 0.2 μm filter, and then vacuum-dried. The resultant substance was dispersed in deionized water (DI) for further research.

Following a standard procedure, MnO2 nanosheets were synthesised by adding 0.32 mL of SDS solution (0.1 M) and 1.6 mL of H2SO4 solution (0.1 M) to 283.2 mL of distilled water at 95 °C for 15 min while stirring continuously. Then, 3.2 mL of a 0.05 M KMnO4 solution was added rapidly to the preceding solution to initiate the reaction at 95 °C for 60 min while stirring continuously. Initially, the KMnO4 solution was violet-red in colour, but it gradually transformed into a dark brown colloidal suspension. The reaction producing MnO2 is shown in the following sequential equations.
Image 1

The resulting suspensions were centrifuged, washed, and purified, and MnO2 was used in experiments as a colloidal suspension. The prepared CDs and nanosheets of MnO2 in liquid form were used to formulate the probe, and their concentration was optimised by varying their volume ratio. The preliminary experiment determined that when initial concentrations of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets were excessively high, the intensity of UV absorbance and luminescence emission intensity were excessively high, and the amount of sampling would result in a substantial error. The CD and MnO2 solutions were diluted, and photoluminescence emission studies were employed to optimize the MnO2 solution concentration in the CD: MnO2 probe.

The probe with a CD:MnO2 concentration of 1:1, which exhibited the greatest luminescence quenching, was finally formulated for further testing. Typically, 1 ml of CD solution and 1 ml of MnO2 nanosheets were sequentially mixed at room temperature with continuous stirring. The resulting solution served as the probe for subsequent detection. After the probe was exposed to H2O2 solutions of varying concentrations for 60 s while being stirred, optical measurements were taken.

Using a Transmission Electron Microscope, the nanostructure and surface morphology of the synthesised nanoparticles were evaluated (TEM; JEOL, JEM-1400). X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Miniflex 600) was used to evaluate the crystallinity and phase structure of the nanomaterials. Cu K radiation of wavelength of 0.15418 nm was utilized. The scans were performed at a rate of 3/min over a 2θ range of 10–80°. The absorption spectroscopy data was obtained using a LAMBDA L602008 spectrometer. Dynamic Light Scattering instrument zetasizer (nano-ZS90 of Malvern) was used for estimating surface charge and average particle size. The photoluminescence measurements were carried out utilising an in-house PL measurement system. A 405 nm CW diode laser was used as the excitation source, and a fibre coupled spectrometer was used to record the emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structures of the CDs were investigated by XRD (Fig. 2(a)), which revealed a broad single diffraction peak around 2θ in the range 21°–23°, which originates from graphitic carbon structure. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) [Fig. 2(b)] and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques revealed the size of the synthesised CDs.

In DLS, the fluctuating intensity of the scattering light,. due to the constant random motion of nanoparticles, is measured. Using an appropriate model fitting the autocorrelation function of the fluctuating intensity, one can obtain the diffusion coefficient of the particles. The diffusion coefficient is used to calculate the hydrodynamic radius of the particle using the Stokes-Einstein equation [47]. The hydrodynamic radius can be interpreted as radius of the equivalent sphere of same diffusivity of the actual particle in the liquid medium. Spherical nanoparticle moving through a liquid would always have a stagnant liquid layer. The measured hydrodynamic radius of particles is always greater than dry core, which is usually observed in TEM. Thus we always observe a higher hydrodynamic radius than the size of the particle observed in TEM [48]. CDs are monodispersed with a quasi-spherical morphology, as depicted in the TEM image of CDs (Fig. 2(c).

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of CDs (Fig. 2 (d)) reveals the chemical bonds and surface functional groups. The peaks at wavenumber ~3460 and ~3313–3353 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, respectively. The vibrational mode at 2930 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C–H bond. In addition, the vibrational peak observed at ~1361 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of C–N, which confirms the presence of nitrogen atom in the as-synthesised CDs [49,50]. A strong vibrational mode observed at 1707 cm−1 is assigned to amide (CO), indicating the formation of amido bond through the reaction of the carboxyl group of malic acid with amino of PEI.

Further, the optical properties of CDs were characterized by UV–VIS absorption and Photoluminescence emission spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the as-prepared CDs shows an optical absorption peak in the UV region with a maximum absorption at 301 nm and a tail extending into the visible range. This is attributed to the n–π* transition of the CO band and π–π* transition of the conjugated CC band. The PL emission spectrum of CD (excitation wavelength 405 nm) is shown in Fig. 3 (b). The observed PL shows a broad emission in the visible range which can be ascribed to the presence of the functional groups C═O and C═N on their surface. This is possible due to abundant structural configurations and newly introduced energy levels into their electronic structures, and consequently result in more electronic transition possibilities [51].

The CD solution appears transparent light yellow colored under daylight and under UV light exposure, it exhibits a broad emission in the visible range. Fig. 3 (c) shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets exhibiting a wide characteristic absorption in the range of 200–700 nm, which makes them excellent fluorescence quenchers. The XRD pattern of synthesised MnO2 nanosheets, shows characteristic 2θ peaks = 12.1°, 24.2°, 36.7°, 66°, which can be indexed to δ -MnO2 (JCPDS no. 18–0802) and indicating a typical lamellar structure.

The TEM images shown in Fig. 4 (a) & (b) reveals the sheet like morphology of the as synthesised MnO2 nanosheets. Fig. 4(c) and (d) shows the TEM micrograph of the optical probe comprising of CDs covered by MnO2 nanosheets.

The combined PL emission of CDs and UV-VIS absorbance of MnO2 nanosheets are shown in Fig. 5 (a) which illustrates a significant overlapping, which results in luminescence quenching in the probe. Fig. 5 (c) shows the combined UV-VIS spectra of CD, CD: MnO2 and MnO2, highlighting the broad absorbance of MnO2. This absorption spectrum also highlights the probability of absorption competition quenching mechanism in CDs on addition of MnO2. The absorption ranges of both CD and MnO2 shows significant overlapping with each other. (Fig. 5(c) which increases the probability of absorption competition phenomenon. On adding MnO2, the total available energy absorbed by CDs decreases, which results in reduced excitation energy, the electrons cannot efficiently transfer to the excited state. Hence, the resultant emission of CDs in the optical probe reduces significantly leading to quenching.

In addition, the zeta potential measurements of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets were found to be +12.4 mV and −13.08 mV, respectively, while the potential of the optical probe was found to be +2.3 mV. The results of zeta potential thereby confirm the electrostatic interaction between the two nanomaterials, which further confirms their close proximity. Fig. 5(b) shows the quenching phenomena in the optical probe on subsequent addition of MnO2 in CD solution.

As expected, the luminescence of the probe with CDs: MnO2 has diminished in comparison to the blank CD, which can be attributed to the significant overlap between the two. The maximum quenching is observed for CD: MnO2 in 1:1 ratio solution. Hence, this ratio was considered optimised as a probe for detection of H2O2. When different molar concentration of H2O2 solution were added in the probe solution, the luminescence intensity initiated to recover with increasing molar concentration of H2O2 (Fig. 6 ), which can be explained in reference to an earlier report, according to which in mild acidic solutions (pH < 6.5), the H2O2 is oxidised to oxygen whereas the MnO2 nano-sheets were broken down and Mn4+ was rapidly reduced into colourless Mn2+, as shown in equation 4 [37,38]. leading to the release of CDs and recovery of their luminescence.
(4)
M n
O 2
+
H 2
O 2
+ 2
H +
→ M
n
2 +
+ 2
H 2
O +
O 2
( g )

In our case the approximate pH value of the optical probe was found to be 5.9, hence it highlights the possibility of the above reaction and on the addition of H2O2 in the optical probe, bubble formation was also observed which further supports the oxygen liberation via the above reaction phenomenon. Furthermore, due to H2O2 addition, the fragmentation of MnO2 minimises the resultant absorbance of MnO2 and thereby the inherent emission of CD reappears. It further suggests the interaction mechanism of CD: MnO2 nanosheets-based optical probe with H2O2.
Fig. 6(b) shows the normalized PL peak intensities at varying H2O2 concentrations which shows an increasing trend with concentration.
Selectivity of turn-off and turn-on based optical probe:

We investigated the selectivity of our probe with H2O2 and the mechanism by which it fragments MnO2 nanosheets and converts Mn4+ to Mn+2 in a mildly acidic medium. As a result, we recorded PL emissions of competitive acidic species to rule out the interference of other acidic compounds. Recording turn-on PL emission of the quenched optical probe for HCl and H2SO4 samples of identical concentrations (1 μM) provided more evidence of the probe's preference for H2O2. We discovered that the recovery intensity was greatest for H2O2 and lowest for H2SO4. (Fig. 7 ).

In case of H2O2, the electrode potential for conversion of H2O2 to O2 is −0.695 V thus it can be oxidised by MnO2 having reduction potential of 1.23 V.
2Cl- ⇌ Cl2(g) + 2e-, E0 = − 1.385V
MnO2 + 4H+ + 2Cl- → Mn2+ + 2H2O + Cl2, E0 = − 0.155V

Thus MnO2 can't oxidise HCl under normal conditions. Also, for oxidation of Cl-by MnO2, EMnO2/Mn2+ must be ≥ 1.385, for that minimum [H+] i. e H+ concentration required is approx. 20 M, which is very difficult to achieve (highly acidic medium required).

Similarly, for case of H2SO4:
MnO2 + 2H+ (from H2SO4) →Mn2+ + H2O + 1/2O2(g)
E0 Mno2 /Mn2+ = 1.23V
E0 H2 O/O2 = −1.23V

But E0 for conversion of double bonded oxygen atom of MnO2 (OMnO) into O2 is slightly less than −1.23 V due to the difference in bond energies of single and double bonds. Thus intra-molecular redox reaction of MnO2 in presence of H2SO4 is thermodynamically and kinetically not feasible under normal conditions. Hence, the PL turn-on intensity in case of HCl and H2SO4 is quite low. Other possible cause could be the interaction of HCl and H2SO4 (ions) with the amine surface functional group of CDs, which can further reduce the recovered luminescence from CDs after MnO2 fragmentation.
Furthermore, the PL turn-on mechanism can be further realized from the Johnson-Mehl-Avrami- Kolmogorov (JMAK) (or simply Avrami) model.

To further understand the PL evolution during the mentioned chemical process a simplified Johnson-Mehl-Avrami- Kolmogorov (JMAK) model is hereby used. The typical JMAK kinetic model explains various nucleation and growth mechanisms involving temperature, time, concentration or other controlling factors [52–55]. With the assumptions that the PL is evolved with multiple dynamic processes during the chemical interactions, the JMAK model can be expressed as,
(5)
I
( x )
= 1 −
∑
i = 1,2
1
exp
(
k i
* x
)
m i

The term ‘m’ is called the Avrami exponent which depends on various factors such as photo bleaching/enhancement, saturation of growth, etc. The coefficient ‘k’ determines the enhancement rate, where m > 0 indicates enhancement, and m < 0 refers to the decay or degradation. In the present case, the model has been applied to study the changes in chemical reaction rates, thereby leading to variation in resultant PL emission intensity as observed in our case. The resultant fitted parameters are m1=(+) 0.39, m2=(+) 4.52, k1= (+) 3.07, k2= (+) 0.07. The values of ‘m’ is indicative that the observed study of optical probe with H2O2 involves interaction mechanism with subsequent enhancement of PL intensity. As H2O2 concentration increases, the corresponding rate of breakdown of MnO2 nano-sheets increases, which increases transformation of Mn+4 to Mn+2. The observed chemical transformation indicating the increase of the resultant turn on emission intensity of the quenched probe.

In this study, we have discussed in detail the interaction mechanism of an optical probe comprising of CD: MnO2 with H2O2, which can be used for detection purposes. The synthesised CDs and MnO2 nanosheets were characterized thoroughly for structural, morphological and optical study. The positively charged CDs and MnO2 nanosheets show electrostatic interaction and the resulting optical probe remains in fluorescence-quenched state by energy transfer, owing to the spectral overlap and competitive absorption mechanism between the two. Finally, the optical probe with quenched luminescence was exposed to different concentration of H2O2 solutions, and luminescence recovery was observed due to the fragmentation of MnO2 nanosheets in presence of H2O2 and liberation of Mn2+ ions. The interaction mechanism based on luminescence turn-off and turn-on, have been described in detail. The obtained PL results of luminescence turn-on have been further realized from the Johnson-Mehl-Avrami- Kolmogorov (JMAK) model. Also, the current study highlighted the potential of cost-effective, selective, and eco-friendly optical probe in luminescence turn-on-based sensing of H2O2 and their detailed underlying interaction mechanism.

Swati Bishnoi: Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Jitendra Nath Acharyya: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. G. Vijaya Prakash: Supervision, Project administration. Sudip K. Pattanayek: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author SB would like to acknowledge IITD for providing Postdoctoral fellowship. JNA acknowledges the DST-INSPIRE fellowship. The authors are grateful to the Central Research facility (CRF), IIT Delhi for TEM and XRD facilities.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical properties of CE-doped srfeo3 perovskites-modified electrodes towards hydrogen peroxide oxidation
